title: OSPF Areas
description: Hierarchical groupings of OSPF routers that limit LSA flooding and reduce SPF computation overhead.
fullExplanation: OSPF uses a two-level hierarchy: the backbone (Area 0) and non-backbone areas. All areas must connect to Area 0 (directly or via virtual links). Within an area, all routers have identical LSDBs. Between areas, only summary routes are exchanged — this dramatically reduces the size of the LSDB and the frequency of SPF recalculations. ABRs (Area Border Routers) connect non-backbone areas to Area 0. ASBRs (AS Boundary Routers) redistribute external routes into OSPF.

areaTypes.description: All areas must connect here. Carries inter-area traffic. Cannot be stub.
Receives all LSA types including external routes (Type 5 LSAs).
No external LSAs (Type 5). Uses default route for external destinations. Reduces LSDB.
Cisco extension. No external or inter-area LSAs. Only default route. Smallest LSDB.
Allows limited external route redistribution via Type 7 LSAs. Converted to Type 5 at ABR.

routerRoles.description: All interfaces in one area. Only knows that area's topology.
Connects two or more areas. Maintains separate LSDB per area. Summarizes routes between areas.
Redistributes external routes (from BGP, static, EIGRP) into OSPF as Type 5 LSAs.
Has at least one interface in Area 0.

keyFacts: Area 0 is mandatory — all other areas connect to it
Virtual link: connect non-backbone area to Area 0 through another area (workaround)
ABR summarizes routes between areas — reduces routing table size
Stub area: replace all Type 5 LSAs with a single default route
OSPF areas reduce SPF computation — topology change in one area doesn't affect others
Maximum recommended area size: ~50-100 routers for stability
